w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Event Schema, Storage Model & Processing Rules
Object / Aggregation / Transformation / Transaction / Association Events

This document defines the EPCIS event store that sits at the heart of the track-and-trace platform — the append-only log of every supply-chain observation that has ever happened to a product or logistic unit the platform knows about.

The four identifier documents (GTIN, GLN, SGTIN, SSCC) define WHAT gets tracked. This document defines HOW WE CAPTURE what happened to them. Every custody transfer, every cold-chain reading, every commissioning and decommissioning, every aggregation and disaggregation, every regulatory decommission — all of it flows into and out of the event store defined here.

The platform uses a UNIFIED event storage model: one physical table (time-partitioned) holding all five EPCIS event types, with the common / queryable fields projected as columns and the full EPCIS 2.0 JSON-LD payload preserved verbatim in a JSONB column. Type-specific and multi-valued fields live in purpose-built child tables for query speed.

All field definitions are grounded in the GS1 EPCIS 2.0 Standard (Ratified June 2022), the Core Business Vocabulary (CBV) 2.0, and the EPCIS + CBV Implementation Guideline (March 2023). The storage model draws on the OpenEPCIS reference implementation (the open-source GS1-compliant EPCIS 2.0 repository) and modern event-sourcing patterns.

EPCIS (Electronic Product Code Information Services) is GS1's flagship data-sharing standard for supply chain visibility. Where the four identifier documents (GTIN, GLN, SGTIN, SSCC) defined the master and instance data — the nouns — EPCIS events are the verbs. An EPCIS event captures, in a standardised form, WHAT happened to WHICH objects, WHEN and WHERE it happened, and WHY it happened (plus, in 2.0, HOW — sensor data).
Architecture decision: single event table with JSONB payload
EPCISEvent as a base type with five subclasses:
. All five share a common base — the event envelope — and add type-specific fields.
We chose the single-table approach over the table-per-subclass alternative. Rationale:
EPCIS event streams are append-only, making them a perfect fit for modern event-sourcing schemas;
the full EPCIS JSON-LD payload lives in a JSONB column so we can always regenerate the wire format 1:1 for regulatory submissions (Tatmeen, EMVO);
common / queryable fields (eventTime, bizStep, disposition, readPoint, action) are projected as typed columns so the hot-path queries stay fast;

multi-valued fields (epcList, childEPCs, bizTransactionList, sourceList, destinationList, sensorReports, quantityList) are normalised into purpose-built child tables so "show me every event involving SGTIN X" is a single indexed query;
the approach is resilient to future EPCIS revisions — when GS1 adds a sixth event type, we add new JSONB fields rather than a new table + new ORM mapping.

This is the pattern used by OpenEPCIS (the reference GS1-compliant open-source EPCIS 2.0 repository, maintained by benelog GmbH who sit on the GS1 core member group that standardised EPCIS 2.0). It is the same pattern used by modern event stores like Eventuous, and it is the path of least surprise for developers coming in from other event-sourcing backgrounds.

The common event envelope: eventID, eventType, eventTime, eventTimeZoneOffset, recordTime, bizStep, disposition, action, persistentDisposition, readPoint, bizLocation, sourceList, destinationList, bizTransactionList, certificationInfo, errorDeclaration, sensorElementList, extensions.

The type-specific fields for each of the five EPCIS 2.0 event types: ObjectEvent (epcList, quantityList, ilmd), AggregationEvent (parentID, childEPCs, childQuantityList), TransformationEvent (inputEPCList, inputQuantityList, outputEPCList, outputQuantityList, transformationID, ilmd), TransactionEvent (epcList, parentID, quantityList — plus bizTransactionList is MANDATORY), AssociationEvent (parentID, childEPCs, childQuantityList).
The error-declaration mechanism (EPCIS 1.2+) for retracting or correcting previously-captured events — including how corrective events are linked back to the original.
The sensor-data mechanism (EPCIS 2.0 sensorElementList) for IoT / cold-chain integration.

The unified PostgreSQL event table with time-partitioning by eventTime, the child tables (event_epc, event_quantity, event_biz_transaction, event_source, event_destination, event_sensor_report, event_ilmd), and the indexing strategy.
Ingestion pipeline validation rules, idempotency via content-addressable eventIDs, and the QUARANTINED / REJECTED paths for invalid events.
Projection relationships: how events drive updates to the SGTIN / SSCC / instance-status tables, and how those tables are derived views of the authoritative event log.
EPCIS capture and query REST API endpoints — covered in the separate EPCIS API specification.
EPCIS subscriptions, webhooks, and streaming delivery to trading partners — covered in the Subscription specification.
The exact ingestion pipeline implementation (Kafka topics, worker pool sizing, back-pressure strategy) — covered in the Ingestion Infrastructure specification.
Regulator-specific event translation (Tatmeen submission format, EMVO Hub format, DSCSA TI/TS/TH) — covered in the per-regulator integration specifications.
Sensor-data time-series store (TimescaleDB / InfluxDB) — covered in the IoT / Time-Series specification.

EPCIS 2.0 defines exactly five standard event types, each modelling a different kind of supply-chain occurrence. The standard also permits an 'ExtendedEvent' type for custom events outside the five — we permit but flag these in audit.
Something happened to one or more
and they all participated in the same way (commissioned, shipped, received, dispensed, destroyed). BY FAR the most common event type.

Objects were physically aggregated (packed) or disaggregated (unpacked). Reversible — each child retains its identity and can be un-aggregated later. Pack-into-case and case-into-pallet are the canonical examples.
One or more input objects were
and one or more output objects were produced. IRREVERSIBLE — inputs cease to exist as identifiable objects. Pharma example: repackaging / kitting.
inputEPCList, inputQuantityList, outputEPCList, outputQuantityList, transformationID, ilmd
Objects are being associated with (or disassociated from) one or more business transactions (POs, invoices, dispatch
). Rarely used in practice — bizTransactionList is present on all other event types too, so a TransactionEvent is only needed when the sole point of the event is the transaction link.
epcList, parentID, quantityList. bizTransactionList is MANDATORY (non-empty).

Long-term association between a parent object and child objects or a location. NEW in EPCIS 2.0. Pharma use: sensor device glued to a cold-chain pallet; component installed in a machine; sensor fixed inside a cold room.
Every EPCIS event captures context along five dimensions. Think of them as the journalistic 5Ws (plus H). All five are expressed in EVERY event type — they are fields on the common base class.
epcList, childEPCs, parentID, inputEPCList, outputEPCList, quantityList, ilmd
Varies by event type — this is WHERE the five types differ most
eventTime = business reality; recordTime = when we stored it. Regulators care about eventTime.
Where did it happen / where are the objects now?
readPoint = point of data capture (specific scanner); bizLocation = business-level location (the plant). Both GLN-based.
What business process / intent is driving this?
bizStep, disposition, action, persistentDisposition, bizTransactionList, sourceList, destinationList, certificationInfo
All CBV-vocabulary-driven. bizStep is the process verb; disposition is the resulting state.
What measurements / conditions accompany the event?
NEW in EPCIS 2.0. Temperature, humidity, pressure, shock. Solves
Our platform implements EPCIS 2.0 natively. These are the key additions worth knowing about for this specification:
JSON-LD wire format added alongside XML (the legacy 1.x format). We use JSON-LD as the canonical payload; XML is still accepted at ingestion and converted to JSON-LD internally.
REST API bindings added alongside SOAP / WSDL (which we do not implement — modern API only).

A NEW event type: AssociationEvent — for long-term associations of objects (sensor attached to a pallet, component installed into a machine). Distinct from AggregationEvent because the association may be permanent or long-lived rather than transient transport-state.
A NEW event dimension: HOW — sensor data via sensorElementList. Solves the cold-chain and IoT integration problem natively in the standard.
New field on the base event: persistentDisposition — solves the 'some dispositions stick, some don't' modelling gap.
New field on the base event: certificationInfo — attach certifications / compliance evidence to any event.
GS1 Digital Link URI syntax adopted alongside classic URN form for all identifiers. Both accepted; we normalise to Digital Link internally.
Events are content-addressable: the optional eventID field uses RFC 6920 ni:// URIs with SHA-256 hashes, enabling integrity verification and trivial idempotency.
EPCIS events are FACTS — you don't edit facts

. Events are snapshots of what the supply chain reported. They are not edited or deleted — instead, a new event is added that corrects or supersedes the prior one. This preserves the full audit trail, which is both a good architectural practice AND a hard regulatory requirement for pharma.
When a prior event turns out to have been erroneous (e.g., reported a destruction that didn't

: a new event is issued with the same content as the original PLUS an errorDeclaration block. Optionally, separate corrective events can be issued and linked back via the correctiveEventIDs list. All three (original, declaration, corrective) live in the log forever.

The platform NEVER UPDATEs or DELETEs event rows. The only writes to the events table are INSERTs. The derived instance tables (SGTIN / SSCC status projections) do get UPDATEd, but they are derived views — they can always be regenerated from the event log.

GS1 EPCIS 2.0 Standard (Ratified June 2022) — §7 Data Definition Layer (base event, five event types, sensor element, error declaration, persistent disposition, certification info); §8 Abstract Data Model Layer; §12 Conformance.

GS1 Core Business Vocabulary (CBV) 2.0 — BizStep vocabulary (~40 values), Disposition vocabulary (~30 values), BusinessTransactionType vocabulary, SourceDestinationType vocabulary, ErrorReason vocabulary, SensorType vocabulary.
GS1 EPCIS and CBV Implementation Guideline (March 2023) — canonical examples, advanced aggregation modelling, class-level tracing, error-declaration walk-throughs.
GS1 EPCIS XML Schema (github.com/gs1/EPCIS / EPCglobal-epcis-2_0.xsd) — authoritative XSD for XML-format events; used for XML→JSON-LD conversion.
) — authoritative JSON-LD context file pinning all standard vocabulary URIs.

OpenEPCIS reference implementation (openepcis.io) — community-edition EPCIS 2.0 repository; validation library; event-hash generator; test data generator. Used as the implementation reference for event-hashing and canonicalisation.
RFC 6920 Naming Things with Hashes — content-addressable ni:// URI scheme used in eventID.
This section describes the physical storage model before diving into field-level specifications. Having the shape of the tables in mind makes the field cards easier to follow.
One row per EPCIS event, all five types. Common fields as typed columns. Full EPCIS JSON-LD as payload JSONB. Time-partitioned by eventTime (monthly).
Envelope queries; regulator submission payload recovery
Many-to-many. One row per (event, EPC) with role: EPC / CHILD / PARENT / INPUT / OUTPUT. Covers epcList, childEPCs, parentID, inputEPCList, outputEPCList.
"All events involving EPC X" — THE critical index
Many-to-many for class-level quantity entries. Covers quantityList, childQuantityList, inputQuantityList, outputQuantityList.
Many-to-many. One row per (event, type, bizTransaction URI).
"All events tied to this PO / invoice / DA"
One row per (event, type, source URI). Types: owning_party, possessing_party, location.
Per-report rows for sensorElementList contents — type, value, uom, min/max/mean, timestamp, deviceID.
One row per (event, ilmd_attribute_name, ilmd_attribute_value). Only populated for ObjectEvent-ADD and TransformationEvent.
"Instance master data at commissioning" — batch, expiry

The EPCIS wire format embeds multiple collections inside a single event (epcList, childEPCs, bizTransactionList, sourceList, destinationList, sensorReports, quantityList). We could leave them inside the JSONB payload and query with Postgres JSONB path operators — but the hot path for this platform is "show me every event involving SGTIN X", "show me every event in a given PO", "show me every event at location Y". These queries are unworkable on JSONB alone at scale.

The solution is to normalise each collection into a dedicated child table with a composite key (event_id, item) and tight indexes. Ingestion fans out each event into N writes (one to `event`, N to `event_epc`, M to `event_biz_transaction`, etc.) inside a single transaction. Reads use the child tables for the selective filters, then join back to `event` for the full
fall back to the JSONB payload only when a caller needs the exact wire format (regulator submissions).
The `event` table is RANGE PARTITIONED on eventTime, one partition per calendar month. Per the platform-level decision:
Hot partitions (last 3 months) live on fast SSD with full indexing.
Warm partitions (3-24 months) remain fully indexed but on slower storage; occasionally compacted.

Cold partitions (24+ months, past retention-expiry for the partition's eventTime range) are detached from the live schema and moved to long-term object storage (S3 Glacier or equivalent) with a manifest. They remain queryable with noticeable latency for audit purposes.
Child tables are similarly partitioned by event_time (via the parent event's partition boundary) to keep joins
Automated partition management: a scheduled job creates the N+2 future partition monthly and archives partitions that exit the retention window. We recommend pg_partman or equivalent.
4. Common Envelope vs Type-Specific Fields — Categorisation

EPCIS events share a substantial common base (the event envelope) and add type-specific fields depending on the event type. This document groups them into the same way — one section for the base envelope (Section 5) and one for the type-specific fields (Section 6).
Yes — part of the base class, present (at least optionally) on all five event types
No — only on the specific event type that defines it
Typed columns on the `event` table plus projection child tables (event_source, event_destination, event_biz_transaction, event_sensor_report)
Normalised into event_epc (with role column distinguishing EPC / CHILD / PARENT / INPUT / OUTPUT), event_quantity, event_ilmd
Stable across EPCIS revisions; EPCIS 2.0 added a few (persistentDisposition, certificationInfo, sensorElementList) and we defined columns for them
Adding a new EPCIS event type (unlikely but possible) = add a new role value + handlers; no schema change needed
18 fields across 5 groups (one per event type)
These fields apply to every EPCIS event regardless of type. They live as typed columns on the unified `event` table (or in purpose-built child tables for multi-valued fields).

Each field card: GS1 / EPCIS 2.0 spec reference, type and length, format, mandatory status (per EPCIS spec AND per platform policy — the platform is stricter than EPCIS in some places), frontend / backend / database validations, sample valid/invalid values, cross-field rules, developer notes.
M (platform-level; EPCIS itself makes it optional but we REQUIRE it)
Show the hex hash prefix for identification, full URI on hover
If the ingested event carries no eventID, COMPUTE one using the OpenEPCIS event-hash algorithm (canonical JSON → SHA-256 → ni URI)
If the event carries an eventID, validate it matches the computed hash (integrity check) OR accept the caller's ID and store the computed hash separately as content_hash
Idempotency: re-ingesting the same eventID MUST be a no-op returning 200 OK (not an error)
255) PRIMARY KEY (within the events table partition)
Index used by the ingestion idempotency check
Platform computes one; recorded event always has an ID
Must be a valid URI per EPCIS spec
Referenced by errorDeclaration.correctiveEventIDs when this event is later declared erroneous.

EPCIS 2.0 formally added the OpenEPCIS event-hash algorithm as the recommended eventID generator. The algorithm canonicalises the event JSON (sorted keys, normalised whitespace, URN↔Web-URI equivalence) then hashes. Use it — it gives you integrity verification for free. Reference impl: github.com/openepcis/openepcis-event-hash-generator.
ObjectEvent / AggregationEvent / TransformationEvent / TransactionEvent / AssociationEvent / ExtendedEvent
Drop-down; event-type-specific form shown after selection
Validate against the EPCIS 2.0 list of event types
Determines which type-specific fields are required in the payload
ExtendedEvent permitted but flagged in the UI — requires a tenant-specific profile
Index on event_type for filtered queries
DEPRECATED in EPCIS 1.1 — subsumed by ObjectEvent with quantityList
Drives which type-specific fields apply (epcList for ObjectEvent, parentID + childEPCs for Aggregation/Association, inputEPCList/outputEPCList for Transformation, etc.).

The QuantityEvent was deprecated in EPCIS 1.1 when quantityList was added to ObjectEvent. Do not implement it. ExtendedEvent is EPCIS 2.0's hook for custom event types outside the five standard ones — rare, but present for completeness.
Default: current time in the user's timezone
Must include timezone offset (not Z-relative) per EPCIS 2.0 requirement
Must be ≤ current server time + small skew tolerance (suggested: 60 seconds)
For commissioning-category events: must be ≥ current time − configurable back-dating tolerance (typical: 7 days)
THIS IS THE PARTITION KEY — monthly range partitions
Space separator not permitted in ISO 8601 wire
Must be ≥ the events that logically precede it for this EPC (e.g., a shipping event can't be before the commissioning event of its epcList).
EventTime is the BUSINESS time (when the thing
); recordTime is the SYSTEM time (when we stored it). Regulators care about eventTime. Use eventTime as the partition key — retention policies and cold-storage archival work on eventTime.
Must match the offset embedded in eventTime (consistency check)
Must be numeric offset, not timezone name
Must be explicit ±HH:MM even for UTC
This is redundant with the offset inside
but EPCIS 2.0 keeps it as a separate field for query convenience — some repositories index it. We store it separately AND keep it inside the payload JSONB.
Read-only; displays when the event landed in the repository
Server-populated at ingestion time using UTC
Never trust caller-supplied recordTime — always overwrite
Used for audit ('when did we learn about this') distinct from eventTime ('when did it happen')
recordTime ≥ eventTime is the common case but NOT required (late-arriving events can have eventTime far in the past).
EPCIS queries support recordTime-based retrieval so a subscriber can catch up on 'new events since my last poll'. Our subscription-delivery worker uses recordTime cursor-based pagination.
CBV short name: commissioning / packing / shipping / receiving / storing / dispensing / etc. OR full URI: https://ref.gs1.org/cbv/BizStep-shipping
O (per EPCIS); M (platform — required for all ingested events)
Drop-down of CBV 2.0 values, grouped by lifecycle stage
Allow custom bizStep (full URI) for extension scenarios but flag it in audit
Canonicalise to compact name form (e.g., 'shipping') for storage
Validate against the CBV 2.0 vocabulary list
Custom values permitted but must be valid URIs and outside the GS1 CBV namespace
Index on biz_step for 'all shipping events in range X' queries
Must be lowercase CBV short name 'shipping'
That's a DISPOSITION value, not a bizStep
Combined with disposition determines workflow transitions. Certain bizStep values require specific action (commissioning → ADD; destroying → DELETE).
The CBV 2.0 BizStep vocabulary has ~40 values. Maintain a lookup table and a migration path when CBV adds new values in future revisions. The full list: github.com/gs1/EPCIS → epcis-
CBV short name: active / in_progress / in_transit / available / dispensed / destroyed / recalled / expired / ...
Drop-down; values filtered by compatible bizStep
Custom URIs permitted; flag in audit
Index on disposition for inventory-status queries
That's a bizStep, not a disposition
Stored on the event AND denormalised onto the affected SGTIN/SSCC instance table (as 'Instance Status' / 'Logistic-Unit Status').
Disposition represents the business state of the object AFTER the event. For SGTIN/SSCC instance tables, we maintain the latest disposition as a denormalised column for fast inventory queries.
Array of CBV disposition short names or URIs
List of dispositions that START being true for the affected objects with this event
Persists across subsequent events until explicitly unset
Example: setting 'completeness_verified' on a pallet at receipt — stays true until proven otherwise
Or normalised into an event_persistent_disposition child table
Unlike 'disposition' (transient — reflects state at event time), persistentDisposition 'set' values accumulate on the object and remain true in subsequent contexts.

NEW in EPCIS 2.0. Solves a longstanding modelling gap: some dispositions are transient (e.g., 'in_transit' stops being true once received) but others stick (e.g., 'damaged' — once damaged, always damaged until explicitly repaired). The 'set' values are the ones that start being true; the 'unset' values are the ones that stop being true.
List of dispositions that STOP being true for the affected objects with this event
Example: unsetting 'damaged' after a successful repair ObjectEvent
M for ObjectEvent, AggregationEvent, TransactionEvent, AssociationEvent. MUST NOT be present for TransformationEvent.
Drop-down; show only for applicable event types
ADD: event marks the beginning of the life of the object(s) — typically commissioning
OBSERVE: object's lifecycle state is unchanged — the event just records an observation
DELETE: event marks the end of the life of the object(s) — typically decommissioning, destruction, dispense
For AggregationEvent: ADD = aggregate children to parent; DELETE = disaggregate; OBSERVE = observe existing aggregation
For TransformationEvent: action field is OMITTED entirely per EPCIS spec
10) CHECK (action IN ('ADD','OBSERVE','DELETE') OR action IS NULL)
Must be ADD, OBSERVE, or DELETE
Action MUST NOT be present for TransformationEvent
that EPC's first life event — must not be preceded by any prior event for that EPC. DELETE claims the last — must not be followed by any subsequent event for that EPC (except errorDeclaration).

The 'no prior event / no subsequent event' rule is not enforced by the wire format — it's semantic. Enforce in the ingestion pipeline via EPC-timeline cross-check. Violations indicate either data quality bugs OR legitimate operational issues (e.g., two systems both claiming to commission the same SGTIN).
O (per EPCIS); M (platform — required for all events)
GLN picker; auto-format into Digital Link URI
Show the GLN's human-readable name next to the picker
Extract GLN from the URI; must exist in core_gln_master
GLN must have Active status at event time
The physical point of data capture (scanning device, workstation, packing-line sensor). More granular than bizLocation.
GLN extension for specific packing line)
Bare GLN without URI wrapper; EPCIS requires a URI
Location must be active at event time
For pharma events: location's Pharma Role (MANUFACTURER / WHOLESALER / PHARMACY / etc.) must be compatible with the bizStep (e.g., dispensing only at DISPENSER).
EPCIS 2.0 accepts both Digital Link (https://id.gs1.org/414/...) and classic URN (
) forms. Normalise to Digital Link internally — it's the 2.0 preferred form. GLN-extension (AI 254) encodes sub-locations below the GLN (specific docks, aisles, lines).
Extract GLN; must exist in core_gln_master and be active
The business-level location (the plant, the warehouse, the pharmacy) — less granular than readPoint
Often same GLN as readPoint for single-site operations; differs when readPoint is at a sub-location level
a parent of readPoint in the location hierarchy.
Cross-check: for custody-transfer events, both source and destination of matching types are typically present
Normalised into child table event_source (event_id, type, source_uri, source_gln)
Paired with destinationList for custody-transfer semantics on shipping/receiving events.

EPCIS 2.0 defines three standard source/destination types: 'owning_party' (legal ownership), 'possessing_party' (physical possession without ownership — e.g., 3PL), 'location' (physical location change). Custom types permitted via full URI.
Normalised into child table event_destination (event_id, type, destination_uri, destination_gln)
O for ObjectEvent / AggregationEvent / AssociationEvent / TransformationEvent; MANDATORY for TransactionEvent (by definition)
type from CBV 2.0 BusinessTransactionType vocabulary: po, inv, desadv, bol, cert, pedigree, prodorder, rma, etc.
bizTransaction is a URI — customer-defined document reference
For TransactionEvent: list MUST be non-empty
Index on (type, biz_transaction_uri) for 'find all events for PO X' queries
For TransactionEvent: this IS the Why dimension's core payload.

CBV 2.0 BusinessTransactionType values: bol, cert, desadv, inv, pedigree, po, poc, prodorder, recadv, rma. Pharma uses especially: po (purchase order), desadv (dispatch advice = ASN), inv (invoice), pedigree (legacy DSCSA-era pedigree document).
Array of URIs pointing to certification documents or certification master data entries
Optionally resolve URIs to validate the certification is active at event time

NEW in EPCIS 2.0 (added to the core EPCISEvent type). Primary use case: sustainability/traceability certifications (organic, fair-trade, deforestation-free). For pharma: GMP/GDP/licensing references for the readPoint / bizLocation at event time.
C (present ONLY when this event declares a prior event as erroneous)
Special event-creation workflow 'Declare prior event in error' — not a standard submission
When present, require a reason and at least one corrective event ID to be recorded
declarationTime: ISO 8601 timestamp, the moment the error was discovered
reason: optional URI from CBV ErrorReason vocabulary (did_not_occur / incorrect_data / other)
correctiveEventIDs: optional list of eventIDs of NEW events issued to remediate the erroneous prior event
Child fields on the events table: err_declaration_time TIMESTAMPTZ, err_reason
Index on err_corrective_event_ids for reverse lookup
The erroneous prior event is identified by its eventID. THIS event retains all original fields (same EPCs, same action, etc.) — it's just tagged with the error declaration.

EPCIS explicitly forbids deleting or modifying events — they're append-only. When an event turns out to be wrong, you issue an ERROR DECLARATION EVENT: same content as the original + errorDeclaration block. Optionally issue separate CORRECTIVE EVENTS that set the record straight. Both remain in the event log forever. The UI should show error-declared events with a distinct visual treatment ('declared in error on <date>, reason: <reason>') and a link to the corrective events.
Typically populated by IoT ingestion pipeline, not hand-entered
Each sensorElement has: sensorMetadata (time, deviceID, deviceMetadata, rawData, dataProcessingMethod, bizRules) and sensorReport (list of
, value, uom, minValue, maxValue, meanValue, sDev,
type values: Temperature, Humidity, Pressure, Illuminance, ... (defined in CBV 2.0 SensorType)
uom from UN/CEFACT Recommendation 20 (CEL for Celsius, P1 for percent, etc.)
Child table: event_sensor_report (event_id, report_seq, sensor_type, value, uom, min_value, max_value, mean_value, s_dev, timestamp, device_id)
Reference to time-series store for the full high-frequency series
— Sensor type must be 'Temperature'; uom must be 'CEL'
For pharma cold-chain: the platform's excursion-detection service scans sensorReport entries and updates Cold-Chain Excursion flag on the affected SGTINs / SSCC.

sensorElementList is the EPCIS 2.0 'How' dimension. It supports high-frequency IoT data and lightweight per-event captures alike. In our platform: small reports (min/max/mean aggregates) live in event_sensor_report; full time-series (reading every 5 minutes across 72-hour transit) live in the dedicated time-series store linked by deviceID.
The complete as-submitted EPCIS event object including @context
Read-only; viewable via a 'raw JSON' toggle in the event viewer
Store EXACTLY the EPCIS JSON received (or the canonicalised form used for hashing)
This is the regulatory source of truth; all denormalised columns are projections of this
Never edit; re-submit via errorDeclaration if incorrect
GIN index on the jsonb column for ad-hoc queries
Compress heavily — these rows are mostly-
and we need billions of them
All other event columns are DERIVED projections of this JSONB; the JSONB is authoritative.

This is the single most important column in the events table. Never let a column value contradict the JSONB — if they disagree, the JSONB wins. The projection columns exist for speed; the JSONB exists for correctness.
Keys must be namespaced (contain a ':') to avoid collisions with future standard fields
Validate against tenant's registered extension schema (if any)
— Must be namespaced with a prefix

EPCIS is deliberately extensible — trading partners often carry custom data (internal work orders, operator IDs, quality readings). Capture but don't assume schema. Extension namespaces should be registered per tenant so the platform can validate and index them.
Server-set at ingestion via the capture API
Same as recordTime in meaning; we keep it as a separate column to decouple: recordTime is part of the EPCIS event and visible to consumers; received_at is the platform internal audit.
Usually received_at == recordTime, but they can differ if we back-date recordTime for bulk historic imports. received_at tells the auditor when the platform
Populated by the ingestion pipeline based on the entry point
Helps diagnose data-quality issues — bulk imports often need different validation leniency than live capture-API events.
Free text — identifies the source system / user / batch
For CAPTURE_API: the API client ID
For BULK_IMPORT: the batch job ID
For PARTNER_SUBSCRIPTION: the partner's subscription ID
Computed using the OpenEPCIS event-hash algorithm over the canonicalised payload
If the event was ingested with a precomputed eventID matching ni:///sha-256;<hash>, the hash portions MUST match
Forms the hex portion of content-addressable eventIDs.
Same algorithm as the GS1-recommended OpenEPCIS event hash generator. Deterministic: re-hashing the same semantic event gives the same hash, even if whitespace or key ordering differs in the JSON.
VALIDATED: passed all validations; available to queries and downstream subscribers
QUARANTINED: passed schema but failed business rules (e.g., ADD on already-existing EPC); held for manual review
REJECTED: schema-invalid events; stored for audit but excluded from query results
Quarantined events are still stored — regulators want ALL submissions logged, not silently discarded.

Regulators (especially Tatmeen) insist on tamper-evident append-only logs. Even rejected events are kept for audit. Queries filter by default to VALIDATED only; admin tooling can show QUARANTINED and REJECTED.

These fields are defined by specific EPCIS event-type subclasses. Each group below corresponds to one event type and lists only the additional fields that event type adds on top of the common envelope.
All type-specific field values are STORED in the `payload` JSONB column (verbatim)
normalised into `event_epc`, `event_quantity`, or `event_ilmd` child tables for indexed lookup.
GS1 Digital Link or EPC URN form per entry
C (ObjectEvent must have at least one of epcList OR quantityList non-empty)
Instance-level EPCs: SGTIN, SSCC, GLN, GRAI, GIAI, etc.
For ADD: no prior events may exist for any EPC in the list
For DELETE: no subsequent events may exist for any EPC in the list (except errorDeclaration)
Normalised into event_epc (event_id, epc_uri, epc_gs1_key, role = 'EPC')
role = 'EPC' distinguishes from CHILD / INPUT / OUTPUT / PARENT
All EPCs participate in the event in the same way (same bizStep, same disposition, same custody change).
If a single physical event involves EPCs that participate DIFFERENTLY (e.g., some shipped, some returned), split into multiple ObjectEvents — one per uniform group.
); quantity: number; uom: optional unit of measure
C (alternative to epcList for class-level tracking)
epcClass is the CLASS form (using idpat URI) rather than instance
Used when serialisation is not present or when the event concerns a batch without naming individual serials
Typical pharma use: bulk receiving of non-serialised OTC product
Normalised into event_quantity (event_id, epc_class, quantity, uom, role = 'QUANTITY')
ObjectEvent must have at least one of epcList OR quantityList non-empty.

Class-level identification appeared in EPCIS 1.1 to accommodate products that aren't serialised at the instance level. For pharma under EU FMD / DSCSA / Tatmeen, instance-level (epcList) is the norm; class-level is mostly relevant for ingredients, OTC, and non-regulated product types.
Key-value pairs using CBV MDA (Master Data Attribute) vocabulary
JSON form with CBV MDA attribute picker
ILMD may be used ONLY in ObjectEvent and TransformationEvent (EPCIS 2.0 §7.4.2 / §7.4.4)
ILMD may be used ONLY when action = ADD (the object is coming into existence)
ilmd JSONB (denormalised snapshot) OR child table event_ilmd (event_id, attr_name, attr_value)
ILMD only permitted on ADD actions (commissioning)
ILMD attributes are STATIC across the life of the object — they describe what the object IS. Dynamic state goes in disposition / bizStep, not ILMD.
GS1 treats ILMD as a snapshot of per-instance master data captured
the instance came into existence. For pharma: lot number and expiry are the canonical ILMD pair, and for Tatmeen submissions the ILMD on the commissioning event IS the way to convey these.
SSCC, SGTIN, or other aggregation-capable GS1 key in URI form
M for action=ADD/DELETE; OPTIONAL for action=OBSERVE (if the children are observed in aggregation but the parent identity is unknown)
an SSCC URI; less commonly an SGTIN (for kits/bundles)
Parent must exist in the relevant instance table (core_sscc_instance or core_sgtin_instance)
Parent's status must be ACTIVE / IN_TRANSIT / RECEIVED (not DISPENSED / DECOMMISSIONED)
Also denormalised onto the event row as parent_epc for fast lookup
Children EPCs become aggregated to / disaggregated from this parent.
M for action=ADD/OBSERVE; OPTIONAL for action=DELETE (if omitted, all children are disaggregated)
Each child must exist in the relevant instance table
No child may already have a different parent at event time (unless this event is a re-aggregation that implicitly disaggregates)
DELETE with empty childEPCs = disaggregate ALL children from the parent (a common 'break down this pallet' operation).
Used when children are class-level (non-serialised) — e.g., bulk totes of non-serialised product aggregated onto a serialised pallet
Normalised into event_quantity (event_id, epc_class, quantity, uom, role = 'CHILD_QUANTITY')
Rare in pharma (which mostly serialises down to the saleable unit); more common in F&B / cosmetics / hardware.
C (at least one of inputEPCList or inputQuantityList must be non-empty)
Each input EPC must exist and be available (not dispensed / destroyed)
Inputs are CONSUMED — they cease to exist as identifiable objects after the transformation (distinguish from aggregation, which is reversible)
Transformation is irreversible — inputs cannot appear in future events (their identity is gone).
Pharma examples: compounding / kitting operations where saleable packs are combined into multi-pack therapy kits; repackaging where original packs are destroyed and new packs emerge.
Used when inputs are class-level (raw materials, ingredients)
C (at least one of outputEPCList or outputQuantityList must be non-empty)
Output EPCs come into existence AT this event
No prior events may reference these EPCs
The output EPCs' commissioning is implicit in this event — no separate commissioning ObjectEvent needed.
O (when used, links multiple related TransformationEvents together)
Used to link TransformationEvents that together form one logical transformation process extended across space or time
Example: a manufacturing process captured as multiple events (raw-material consumption → WIP staging → final-product creation) all sharing one transformationID
Index on transformation_id for process-level queries
Events sharing a transformationID may omit EITHER the inputs OR the outputs (the union of all such events makes the full mass balance).
Rarely used in simple pharma flows but important for complex compounding / contract-manufacturing scenarios.
ILMD on TransformationEvent describes the OUTPUT objects (since they are coming into existence)
Outputs inherit this ILMD as their instance-level master data
On TransformationEvent the action field is OMITTED (per EPCIS spec) because the lifecycle action is implicit: inputs are DELETE, outputs are ADD.
C (at least one of epcList or quantityList must be non-empty)
EPCs being associated with or disassociated from the business transactions listed in bizTransactionList
Optional parent EPC — if present, the event concerns the parent AND its direct children implicitly
an SSCC when the whole logistic unit is being associated with a transaction
Less-common pattern; many pharma implementations put the pallet SSCC in epcList directly rather than as parentID.
E. AssociationEvent — Additional Fields (NEW in EPCIS 2.0)
EPC of the parent (or location URI)
M for action=ADD/DELETE; OPTIONAL for action=OBSERVE
Typical pharma use: SSCC parent with a datalogger deviceID as child
Alternative: location (SGLN) as parent for installing a sensor in a cold room
https://id.gs1.org/414/6291000050000 (SGLN — for sensor installed in a location)

AssociationEvent is EPCIS 2.0's new event type. It differs from AggregationEvent in two important ways: (1) children (typically sensors) can be long-term associated without being transported WITH the parent; (2) the association is typically more permanent (glued-on sensor, installed component).
EPCs of the children (often sensor GIAIs)
M for action=ADD/OBSERVE; OPTIONAL for action=DELETE
Common pharma use: sensor device GIAIs being associated with a cold-chain SSCC
Rare — class-level children associated with the parent

The event log is the source of truth. But day-to-day queries against the platform — "where is SGTIN X now?", "what's the status of pallet SSCC Y?", "give me the inventory at GLN Z" — should not require walking the event log. Instead, the platform maintains derived INSTANCE TABLES (core_sgtin_instance, core_sscc_instance, and their pharma extensions) that are kept in sync with the event log via projection handlers.
Each validated event is dispatched to zero or more projection handlers. Each handler reads the event and applies an UPDATE to the relevant instance table. Handlers are idempotent (keyed on eventID
so replay is safe. Handlers run in the same database transaction as the event
so projection lag is zero during normal operation.
gtin, serial_number, epc_uri, instance_status=COMMISSIONED, commissioned_at, commissioning_readpoint_gln, commissioning_event_id, batch_number, expiration_date (from ILMD)
Creates the SGTIN instance row. Source of truth for per-pack identity.
child SGTIN.parent_epc, aggregated_at; parent SSCC.child_count, total_leaf_count
For each child EPC in childEPCs, link to parent.
Disaggregate children from parent. If childEPCs omitted, disaggregate ALL.
instance_status=IN_TRANSIT, latest_disposition=in_transit, current_custodian_gln updated from destinationList
If excursion detected, propagate flag to every aggregated SGTIN in the subtree.
Original event's effects on instance tables MAY need to be reverted by corrective events
Platform does NOT auto-revert the prior event's projection — requires explicit corrective events.

The derived instance tables can be FULLY REGENERATED from the event log at any time. This is the ultimate disaster-recovery mechanism: truncate the instance tables, replay events in eventTime order, and arrive at
state. Regulators (especially for DSCSA saleable-returns verification and EU FMD audit) appreciate this property.
A full replay for a medium-volume tenant (~100M events) completes in 2-6 hours on the baseline hardware profile — fast enough to be useful; slow enough that we don't use it as the normal update path.
Each rule should be a named validator in the ingestion pipeline. The prefix XE = 'cross-field EPCIS event'. Rules that fail DEFAULT to QUARANTINED ingestion status (not REJECTED
so the regulator still sees the submission attempt; the ops team then reviews and either accepts or issues a correction.
JSON Schema validation against EPCIS 2.0 JSON Schema MUST pass. Schema-invalid events are REJECTED (not quarantined).
Event type must be one of: ObjectEvent, AggregationEvent, TransformationEvent, TransactionEvent, AssociationEvent, ExtendedEvent.
eventID idempotency check: if already present, return success without side effects.
Compute content hash; if eventID is of the form ni:///sha-256;<hash>, verify the hash matches. On mismatch, QUARANTINE.
eventTime must include a timezone offset; must be ≤
eventTimeZoneOffset must match the timezone offset embedded in eventTime.
The `action` field MUST be absent. If present, REJECT.
`action` MUST be one of ADD / OBSERVE / DELETE.
At least one of epcList or quantityList must be non-empty.
ILMD is only permitted when action = ADD. Otherwise QUARANTINE.
parentID MUST NOT appear in childEPCs (no self-reference).
At least one of inputEPCList/inputQuantityList AND at least one of outputEPCList/outputQuantityList must be non-empty (unless transformationID links to sibling events that carry the missing side).
bizStep, disposition, persistentDisposition values must be from CBV 2.0 vocabulary OR a custom URI; log custom URIs to an audit trail for review.
readPoint / bizLocation URIs must resolve to a GLN that exists in core_gln_master and is ACTIVE at eventTime.
Every EPC in epcList / parentID / childEPCs / inputEPCList / outputEPCList must have a valid URI form and parse into a recognised GS1 key type.
For each EPC in epcList: no prior event may exist for this EPC. If one exists: QUARANTINE and flag potential duplicate commissioning.
For each EPC in epcList: no subsequent event (other than errorDeclaration) may follow. Enforce by marking the EPC as terminal after this event.
For owning_party / possessing_party entries, URI MUST be a PGLN (417). For location, URI MUST be an SGLN (414).
The JSONB payload's canonicalisation MUST round-trip: canonicalise → hash → re-canonicalise → re-hash → same hash.
errorDeclaration.declarationTime must be ≥ the original event's recordTime.
Extension namespace prefixes in custom fields must be declared in the
Each sensorReport must carry a `type` from CBV SensorType and a numeric `value` + `uom` from UN/CEFACT Rec 20.
An event with eventTime > 24 hours earlier than recordTime is flagged for review (may be legitimate bulk-import but is unusual for live capture).
Events for pharma instances must include readPoint AND bizLocation AND bizStep AND disposition (stricter than EPCIS's optional rules — regulators need them).
One worked example of each of the five EPCIS 2.0 event types, shown as JSON-LD. These are the exact payloads the platform accepts and stores in the `payload` JSONB column.
— pack three SGTINs into a case SSCC
— associate SGTINs with a purchase order
— attach a datalogger to a pallet
declaration — retracting a prior (erroneous) destroying event

Full DDL for the event storage model. Designed for: high-throughput INSERTs (commissioning events arrive in bulk from packaging lines); indexed EPC-based retrieval (by far the dominant read pattern); JSONB-based wire-format reproduction (for regulator submissions); time-based archival.
CHECK (action IS NULL OR action IN ('ADD','OBSERVE','DELETE')),
-- partition key must be in PK
CREATE TABLE event_2026_04 PARTITION OF event
CREATE TABLE event_2026_05 PARTITION OF event
CREATE INDEX idx_event_type_time ON event (event_type, event_time DESC
CREATE INDEX idx_event_bizstep ON event (biz_step, event_time DESC
CREATE INDEX idx_event_read_point ON event (read_point_gln, event_time DESC
CREATE INDEX idx_event_biz_location ON event (biz_location_gln, event_time DESC
-- JSONB payload GIN index for ad-hoc exploration
CREATE INDEX idx_event_payload_gin ON event USING GIN (payload jsonb_path_ops
20) NOT NULL -- SGTIN / SSCC / GLN / GIAI / GRAI
CREATE INDEX idx_event_epc_uri ON event_epc (epc_uri, event_time DESC
CREATE INDEX idx_event_epc_key ON event_epc (epc_key_type, gs1_key, event_time DESC
CREATE INDEX idx_event_epc_role ON event_epc (role, event_time DESC
-- po, inv, desadv, bol, cert, pedigree, ...
CREATE INDEX idx_event_source_gln ON event_source (src_gln, event_time DESC
CREATE INDEX idx_event_dest_gln ON event_destination (dst_gln, event_time DESC
CREATE INDEX idx_event_sensor_device ON event_sensor_report (device_id, reading_time DESC
CREATE INDEX idx_event_sensor_type ON event_sensor_report (sensor_type, reading_time DESC
CREATE INDEX idx_event_ilmd_attr ON event_ilmd (attr_name, attr_value
-- Q1. Full history of one SGTIN (the single most common query)
-- Q2. All events at a location
-- Q3. All events attached to a purchase order
-- Q4. Current children of a pallet (aggregation tree walk)
-- Q5. Regenerate the exact EPCIS JSON-LD for regulator submission
SELECT payload FROM event WHERE event_id = '
Items below need product-team / architecture input before this specification can be locked for implementation.

Canonical event-hash algorithm: confirm we use the OpenEPCIS event-hash generator exactly (github.com/openepcis/openepcis-event-hash-generator) rather than rolling our own. This ensures interop with other EPCIS repositories.
Partition width: monthly is proposed. For tenants expecting 100M+ events/month, weekly partitions may be preferable; for low-volume tenants, quarterly. Should partition width be tenant-configurable?

Multi-tenancy: confirm tenant isolation strategy. Options: (a) tenant_id column on every row + row-level security; (b) schema-per-tenant; (c) database-per-tenant. Each affects partition management and scale limits.
XML ingestion: EPCIS 1.x XML and EPCIS 2.0 XML are both still valid wire formats. Confirm we convert XML → JSON-LD at ingestion (normalise to one form) rather than storing both.

Validation strictness: confirm the QUARANTINE vs REJECT policy per rule. We default to QUARANTINE (event stored, held for review) for business-rule failures and REJECT for schema-invalid events. Regulators may want stricter — some want every submission recorded regardless.

Error-declaration projection: when a prior event is declared in error, should the platform AUTO-REVERT the projection onto the instance tables, or require explicit corrective events? We recommend explicit corrective events — simpler semantics, clearer audit trail.

Retention windows: confirm the archival policy exit-timing per regime. EU FMD requires at least 5 years past decommissioning. DSCSA requires 6 years past transaction. Tatmeen TBD. Use the max across applicable regimes per event.
Cold storage format: confirm archived partition format — pg_dump SQL, Parquet, or JSONL. Parquet gives best query performance for later audits.
Subscription catch-up: confirm cursor-based pagination on recordTime for subscriber backfill. recordTime is stable (never changes after insert); eventTime is not (late-arrivers insert 'in the past').
Ingestion throughput target: confirm the design target (events/sec sustained, events/sec peak). Drives the worker-pool sizing and whether we batch INSERTs via COPY.
Content-hash mismatch behaviour: confirm that eventID hash mismatch is a QUARANTINE (our default) rather than REJECT. Some implementations treat it as an integrity breach and reject outright.
ExtendedEvent support: confirm whether tenant-specific custom event types are permitted at ingestion. Our default permits them with audit flagging — some deployments may want strict five-types-only.

Bulk-import validation: bulk historical imports often violate the 'no prior ADD / no subsequent DELETE' rules because they're backfilling ordered events out of order. Confirm special bulk-import lenient mode that validates at batch-end rather than per-row.
